MANDATORY SETTLEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Apr-02-2021 Off Calendar

Defendant Jennifer Andary'S Motion To Quash Deposition Subpoena For Production Of Business Records
Off Calendar Feb-11-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: Defendant Jennifer Andary's Motion To Quash Deposition Subpoena For Production Of Business Records.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant Jennifer Andary's motion is DENIED.

Plaintiffs Archana Singh and Bhanu Scioscia claim, rightly, that the motion is untimely in that Ms. Andary filed it four days before the extended deadline for Airbnb to produce records in response to the subject subpoena. A motion to quash a subpoena for the production of documents must be served and noticed on opposing counsel at least five days before the date of production of documents. (Code of Civ. Proc., Sec. 1985.3(g)). The five day limit is not jurisdictional; the court is empowered to grant a motion to quash even after the date set for production. (Slagle v. Superior Court (Maryon) (1989) 211 Cal.App.3d 1309, 1312-1313.) However, Ms. Andary has not presented any evidence or argument as to why the court should entertain her untimely motion. Even so, the grounds she presents for quashing the motion are unavailing as follows:

1) Statement of materiality: A deposition subpoena that commands only the production of business records for copying need not be accompanied by an affidavit or declaration showing good cause for the production of the business records designated in it. Code Civ. Proc. Sec. 2020.410(c). Ms. Andary's reliance on McClatchy Newspapers v. Superior Court (1945) 26 Cal.2d 386 is misplaced as that case predated the Discovery Act and did not concern a deposition subpoena issued pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 2020.010 et seq.

2) Reasonableness of the document demands: Ms. Andary contends the subpoena is burdensome and oppressive because the time frame encompasses some thirteen years of records. On the other hand, she makes no showing of burden or oppression as to the actual amount of records implicated by the subpoena or the production of same. There is likewise no indication in the record that the recipient of the subpoena, Airbnb, believes compliance with the subpoena would be burdensome or oppressive.

3) Privacy rights of Ms. Andary and third parties: Ms. Andary also claims the subpoena will infringe on her privacy rights. The subpoena seeks business records which are directly relevant to the action; the records, to the extent they exist, would support the requesting parties' contention that Ms. Andary has carried on a short-term rental business in violation of the CC&Rs. Under the facts presented, Ms. Andary has not articulated a reasonable expectation of privacy, nor does the alleged invasion of privacy constitute an egregious breach of social norms. Pioneer Electronics (USA), Inc. v. Superior Court (2007) 40 Cal. 4th 360, 370-371; see Hill v. Nat'l Collegiate Athletic Assn. (1994) 7 Cal. 4th 1, 35; see also Alch v. Superior Court (2008) 165 Cal. App. 4th 1412, 1423. She has also not explained how she has standing to assert a privacy rights claim on behalf of various unidentified third parties. Meanwhile, the countervailing interests of Ms. Singh and Mr. Scioscia clearly outweigh Ms. Andary's privacy interests.

Court to review prevailing party's proposed order.

Judge Pro Tem: Peter Vestal; Clerk: Sean Kane; Not Reported (302/JPT)
